commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.

learningObjectives.text: Explain model, view, and controller responsibilities using a new example and justify the result.
Explain separation of concerns using a new example and justify the result.
Trace and explain publisher–subscriber event flow using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Model, view, and controller responsibilities** → **Separation of concerns** → **Publisher–subscriber event flow**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: Separation of concerns is best understood as follows: Separate code by responsibility so one kind of change causes minimal unrelated edits. Good boundaries reduce coupling without creating empty abstractions.
choices.label: Separate code by responsibility so one kind of change causes minimal unrelated edits. Good boundaries reduce coupling without creating empty abstractions.

choices.feedback: Publisher–subscriber event flow is best understood as follows: Publisher–subscriber communication lets one part announce an event without directly knowing every consumer. Subscribers respond through a documented event and payload contract.
choices.label: Publisher–subscriber communication lets one part announce an event without directly knowing every consumer. Subscribers respond through a documented event and payload contract.

choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Model, view, and controller responsibilities, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture.
choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture.
choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture.
choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.
questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture.
choices.label: Avoid MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture.
choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.

choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
choices.label: Use MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture.
choices.label: Use MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture in every file because more abstraction is always better.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture?

sections.body: Model owns data, view renders, and controller coordinates input.
sections.title: Model, view, and controller responsibilities

sections.body: Separate code by responsibility so one kind of change causes minimal unrelated edits. Good boundaries reduce coupling without creating empty abstractions.
sections.title: Separation of concerns

sections.body: Publisher–subscriber communication lets one part announce an event without directly knowing every consumer. Subscribers respond through a documented event and payload contract.
sections.title: Publisher–subscriber event flow

sections.explanation: The view publishes intent, the controller coordinates, and the model owns data.
sections.title: Worked example

summary: MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture connects Model, view, and controller responsibilities, Separation of concerns, Publisher–subscriber event flow.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: MVC and Publisher–Subscriber Architecture

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns mvc and publisher–subscriber architecture from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Application Architecture: Forkify** module.
